docs.title: Data visualization
docs.text:
In [ ]: Copied! 
# %pip install geoai-py
# %pip install geoai-py In [ ]: Copied! 
from torchgeo.datasets import NAIP
from torchgeo.samplers import RandomGeoSampler, GridGeoSampler
from geoai.utils import view_image, view_raster, dict_to_image
from geoai.download import download_naip
from torchgeo.datasets import NAIP from torchgeo.samplers import RandomGeoSampler, GridGeoSampler from geoai.utils import view_image, view_raster, dict_to_image from geoai.download import download_naip In [ ]: Copied! 
root = "naip_data"
root = "naip_data" In [ ]: Copied! 
bbox = (-117.6029, 47.65, -117.5936, 47.6563)
downloaded_files = download_naip(
    bbox=bbox,
    output_dir=root,
    max_items=1,
)
bbox = (-117.6029, 47.65, -117.5936, 47.6563) downloaded_files = download_naip( bbox=bbox, output_dir=root, max_items=1, )
  • torchgeo.datasets.NAIP: Provides access to the National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) dataset, which offers high-resolution aerial imagery across the United States.
  • torchgeo.samplers: Contains sampling strategies for geospatial data:
    • RandomGeoSampler: Samples random patches from the dataset
    • GridGeoSampler: Samples patches in a grid pattern with specified stride
  • geoai.utils: Custom utility functions for visualization:
    • view_image: Visualizes tensor images
    • view_raster: Displays georeferenced data on an interactive map
    • dict_to_image: Converts dictionary representation to image format

Load the NAIP dataset from the specified root directory:

In [ ]: Copied! 
dataset = NAIP(root)
dataset = NAIP(root)

Examine the dataset object to understand its properties:

In [ ]: Copied! 
dataset
dataset

This will display information about the NAIP dataset including available imagery dates, coverage area, and other metadata.

Check the Coordinate Reference System (CRS) used by the dataset:

In [ ]: Copied! 
dataset.crs
dataset.crs

The CRS defines how the geospatial data is projected onto a coordinate system, which is essential for accurate visualization and analysis.

In [ ]: Copied! 
train_sampler = RandomGeoSampler(dataset, size=256, length=1000)
train_sampler = RandomGeoSampler(dataset, size=256, length=1000)

This creates a sampler that will randomly select 1000 patches, each 256x256 pixels in size. This sampling strategy is commonly used for training machine learning models where you need a diverse set of examples.

Extract a bounding box from the random sampler:

In [ ]: Copied! 
train_bbox = next(iter(train_sampler))
train_bbox
train_bbox = next(iter(train_sampler)) train_bbox

The bounding box contains the coordinates defining the spatial extent of our randomly sampled patch.

Load the actual image data corresponding to the randomly selected bounding box:

In [ ]: Copied! 
train_image = dataset[next(iter(train_sampler))]["image"]
train_image = dataset[next(iter(train_sampler))]["image"]

Examine the complete data dictionary returned for a sample, which includes both the image and metadata:

In [ ]: Copied! 
dataset[next(iter(train_sampler))]
dataset[next(iter(train_sampler))]

This returns a dictionary containing the image tensor and associated metadata such as the bounding box, CRS, and other properties.

In [ ]: Copied! 
view_image(
    train_image, transpose=True, scale_factor=(1 / 250), title="Random GeoSampler"
)
view_image( train_image, transpose=True, scale_factor=(1 / 250), title="Random GeoSampler" )
  • transpose=True: Rearranges the dimensions for proper display (from [C,H,W] to [H,W,C])
  • scale_factor=(1/250): Scales the pixel values for better visualization
  • title="Random GeoSampler": Adds a descriptive title to the plot
In [ ]: Copied! 
test_sampler = GridGeoSampler(dataset, size=256, stride=128)
test_sampler = GridGeoSampler(dataset, size=256, stride=128)

This sampler extracts 256x256 pixel patches in a grid pattern with a stride of 128 pixels, meaning patches will overlap by 128 pixels. Grid sampling is typically used for inference or testing, where systematic coverage of the area is important.

Extract a bounding box from the grid sampler:

In [ ]: Copied! 
test_bbox = next(iter(test_sampler))
test_bbox
test_bbox = next(iter(test_sampler)) test_bbox

Load the image data for a patch selected by the grid sampler:

In [ ]: Copied! 
test_image = dataset[next(iter(test_sampler))]["image"]
test_image = dataset[next(iter(test_sampler))]["image"] In [ ]: Copied! 
view_image(test_image, transpose=True, scale_factor=(1 / 250), title="Grid GeoSampler")
view_image(test_image, transpose=True, scale_factor=(1 / 250), title="Grid GeoSampler")

The visualization shows a systematically sampled patch from the dataset.

In [ ]: Copied! 
data = dataset[next(iter(test_sampler))]
data = dataset[next(iter(test_sampler))]

Visualize the raster data on an interactive map with Esri.WorldImagery imagery as the background:

In [ ]: Copied! 
view_raster(data, basemap="Esri.WorldImagery")
view_raster(data, basemap="Esri.WorldImagery")

This interactive visualization places the sampled data in its real-world geographic context, allowing you to see how it aligns with the Esri.WorldImagery imagery.

docs.title: Data visualization¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to work with geospatial imagery data using TorchGeo and GeoAI. We'll explore how to load data, sample it using different strategies, and visualize the results.

docs.title: Install Package¶
docs.text: To use the geoai-py package, ensure it is installed in your environment. Uncomment the command below if needed.

docs.title: Importing Required Libraries¶
docs.text: First, we import the necessary libraries for our geospatial data visualization workflow:

docs.title: Random Sampling of Geospatial Data¶
docs.text: Create a random sampler to extract patches from the dataset:

docs.title: Visualizing Randomly Sampled Data¶
docs.text: Display the randomly sampled image:

docs.title: Grid Sampling of Geospatial Data¶
docs.text: Create a grid sampler to extract patches in a systematic pattern:

docs.title: Visualizing Grid Sampled Data¶
docs.text: Display the image from the grid sampler:

docs.title: Advanced Visualization with Geospatial Context¶
docs.text: Load a complete data sample including all metadata:

docs.title: Key Takeaways¶
docs.text:
1. TorchGeo provides a flexible framework for working with geospatial datasets like NAIP.
  2. Different sampling strategies (random vs. grid) serve different purposes in geospatial machine learning workflows.
  3. Visualization tools help understand the data in both pixel space (view_image) and geographic space (view_raster).
  4. Working with geospatial data requires attention to coordinate reference systems (CRS) and proper handling of georeferenced data.

docs.text: These modules allow downloading NAIP imagery and extracting building data statistics.

docs.title: Define Bounding Box¶
docs.text: Define the geographic extent (longitude and latitude) for data downloads.

docs.title: Download NAIP Imagery¶
docs.text: Fetch NAIP aerial imagery for the specified bounding box. The max_items parameter limits the number of downloaded files.

docs.title: Download Building Data¶
docs.text: Retrieve building footprint data in GeoJSON format within the bounding box. The verbose flag provides detailed output.

docs.title: Extract Building Statistics¶
docs.text: If the building data file is successfully downloaded, extract and display relevant statistics such as area, count, and footprint details.

docs.title: Instance Segmentation with GeoAI¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to use the new instance segmentation functionality in GeoAI for training models and running inference on geospatial data.

docs.title: Overview¶
docs.text:
Instance segmentation combines object detection and semantic segmentation to identify and segment individual objects in images. This is particularly useful for:

  • Building detection and segmentation
  • Vehicle counting and tracking
  • Infrastructure mapping
  • Object delineation in satellite imagery

docs.title: New Functions¶
docs.text: GeoAI now provides clear wrapper functions for instance segmentation:

docs.title: Training¶
docs.text: • train_instance_segmentation_model() - Train a Mask R-CNN model

docs.title: Inference¶
docs.text:
• instance_segmentation() - Run inference on a single GeoTIFF
  • instance_segmentation_batch() - Run inference on multiple GeoTIFFs

docs.title: Model Creation¶
docs.text: • get_instance_segmentation_model() - Create a Mask R-CNN model with custom parameters

docs.title: Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Setup¶
docs.text: First, let's check our environment and set up paths.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: We'll use the same dataset as the semantic segmentation example for consistency.

docs.title: Create training data¶
docs.text: We'll create training tiles for instance segmentation. Note that for instance segmentation, we need to ensure each building instance has a unique pixel value in the label.

docs.title: 1. Model Creation¶
docs.text: Let's create an instance segmentation model with custom parameters.

docs.title: 2. Training Instance Segmentation Model¶
docs.text: Now let's train the instance segmentation model using our prepared data.

docs.title: 3. Running Inference¶
docs.text: Once we have a trained model, we can run inference on new images.

docs.title: 4. Vectorize and Visualize Results¶
docs.text: Convert the predicted mask to vector format for better visualization and analysis.

docs.title: 8. Key Parameters Guide¶
docs.text: Here are the key parameters you can adjust:

docs.title: Summary¶
docs.text:
This notebook demonstrated the new instance segmentation functionality in GeoAI:

  1. Model Creation: Created Mask R-CNN models with custom parameters
  2. Training: Trained an instance segmentation model on building data
  3. Inference: Ran inference on test images
  4. Visualization: Converted results to vectors and visualized them
  5. Analysis: Compared with semantic segmentation approaches

The new functions provide a cleaner API while maintaining backward compatibility with existing code. They're built on top of the robust MaskRCNN implementation already present in GeoAI.

docs.title: Available Functions:¶
docs.text:
• geoai.train_instance_segmentation_model() - Train Mask R-CNN models
  • geoai.instance_segmentation() - Single image inference
  • geoai.instance_segmentation_batch() - Batch processing
  • geoai.get_instance_segmentation_model() - Create custom models

)
print("Weights-only training completed!")
# Load weights only (no training state) print("Loading model weights only (not resuming training state)...") geoai.train_segmentation_model( images_dir=f"{out_folder}/images", labels_dir=f"{out_folder}/labels", output_dir=f"{out_folder}/weights_only_training", architecture="unet", encoder_name="resnet34", encoder_weights="imagenet", num_channels=3, num_classes=2, batch_size=4, num_epochs=15, # Train for 15 epochs starting from epoch 0 learning_rate=0.001, val_split=0.2, save_best_only=False, verbose=True, plot_curves=True, checkpoint_path=checkpoint_path, resume_training=False, # Don't resume training state ) print("Weights-only training completed!") In [ ]: Copied! 
# Test inference with resumed model
masks_path = f"{out_folder}/test_prediction.tif"
model_path = f"{out_folder}/resumed_training/best_model.pth"

if os.path.exists(model_path):
    print(f"Running inference with model: {model_path}")
    geoai.semantic_segmentation(
        input_path=test_raster_path,
        output_path=masks_path,
        model_path=model_path,
        architecture="unet",
        encoder_name="resnet34",
        num_channels=3,
        num_classes=2,
        window_size=512,
        overlap=256,
        batch_size=4,
    )
    print(f"Inference completed. Results saved to: {masks_path}")
else:
    print(f"Model not found: {model_path}")

Results saved to: {masks_path}") else: print(f"Model not found: {model_path}") In [ ]: Copied! 
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load training histories
initial_history_path = f"{out_folder}/initial_training/training_history.pth"
resumed_history_path = f"{out_folder}/resumed_training/training_history.pth"
weights_only_history_path = f"{out_folder}/weights_only_training/training_history.pth"

histories = {}
for name, path in [
    ("Initial (10 epochs)", initial_history_path),
    ("Resumed (10→20 epochs)", resumed_history_path),
    ("Weights Only (15 epochs)", weights_only_history_path),
]:
    if os.path.exists(path):
        histories[name] = torch.load(path)
        print(f"Loaded {name}: {len(histories[name]['train_losses'])} epochs")
    else:
        print(f"History not found: {path}")
import torch import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Load training histories initial_history_path = f"{out_folder}/initial_training/training_history.pth" resumed_history_path = f"{out_folder}/resumed_training/training_history.pth" weights_only_history_path = f"{out_folder}/weights_only_training/training_history.pth" histories = {} for name, path in [ ("Initial (10 epochs)", initial_history_path), ("Resumed (10→20 epochs)", resumed_history_path), ("Weights Only (15 epochs)", weights_only_history_path), ]: if os.path.exists(path): histories[name] = torch.load(path) print(f"Loaded {name}: {len(histories[name]['train_losses'])} epochs") else: print(f"History not found: {path}") In [ ]: Copied! 
# Plot comparison - showing the continuation clearly
if histories:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

    # Get the individual histories
    initial_history = histories["Initial (10 epochs)"]
    resumed_history = histories["Resumed (10→20 epochs)"]
    weights_only_history = histories["Weights Only (15 epochs)"]

    # Training Loss - show continuation clearly
    initial_epochs = range(1, 11)
    axes[0].plot(
        initial_epochs,
        initial_history["train_losses"],
        label="Initial (epochs 1-10)",
        marker="o",
        markersize=5,
        color="blue",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=3,
    )

    # Plot resumed training continuation (epochs 11-20 only)
    resumed_epochs = range(11, 21)
    resumed_continuation = resumed_history["train_losses"][10:]  # epochs 11-20
    axes[0].plot(
        resumed_epochs,
        resumed_continuation,
        label="Resumed (epochs 11-20)",
        marker="s",
        markersize=5,
        color="orange",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=2,
    )

    # Plot weights only
    weights_epochs = range(1, 16)
    axes[0].plot(
        weights_epochs,
        weights_only_history["train_losses"],
        label="Weights Only (epochs 1-15)",
        marker="^",
        markersize=4,
        color="green",
        linewidth=2,
        alpha=0.7,
        zorder=1,
    )

    # Add continuation line
    axes[0].plot(
        [10, 11],
        [initial_history["train_losses"][-1], resumed_continuation[0]],
        color="red",
        linewidth=2,
        linestyle="--",
        alpha=0.7,
        label="Continuation",
    )

    axes[0].set_title("Training Loss", fontsize=14)
    axes[0].set_xlabel("Epoch")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Loss")
    axes[0].legend()
    axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

    # Validation IoU
    axes[1].plot(
        initial_epochs,
        initial_history["val_ious"],
        label="Initial (epochs 1-10)",
        marker="o",
        markersize=5,
        color="blue",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=3,
    )

    resumed_iou_continuation = resumed_history["val_ious"][10:]
    axes[1].plot(
        resumed_epochs,
        resumed_iou_continuation,
        label="Resumed (epochs 11-20)",
        marker="s",
        markersize=5,
        color="orange",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=2,
    )

    axes[1].plot(
        weights_epochs,
        weights_only_history["val_ious"],
        label="Weights Only (epochs 1-15)",
        marker="^",
        markersize=4,
        color="green",
        linewidth=2,
        alpha=0.7,
        zorder=1,
    )

    axes[1].plot(
        [10, 11],
        [initial_history["val_ious"][-1], resumed_iou_continuation[0]],
        color="red",
        linewidth=2,
        linestyle="--",
        alpha=0.7,
        label="Continuation",
    )

    axes[1].set_title("Validation IoU", fontsize=14)
    axes[1].set_xlabel("Epoch")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("IoU")
    axes[1].legend()
    axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

    # Validation Dice
    axes[2].plot(
        initial_epochs,
        initial_history["val_dices"],
        label="Initial (epochs 1-10)",
        marker="o",
        markersize=5,
        color="blue",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=3,
    )

    resumed_dice_continuation = resumed_history["val_dices"][10:]
    axes[2].plot(
        resumed_epochs,
        resumed_dice_continuation,
        label="Resumed (epochs 11-20)",
        marker="s",
        markersize=5,
        color="orange",
        linewidth=3,
        alpha=0.9,
        zorder=2,
    )

    axes[2].plot(
        weights_epochs,
        weights_only_history["val_dices"],
        label="Weights Only (epochs 1-15)",
        marker="^",
        markersize=4,
        color="green",
        linewidth=2,
        alpha=0.7,
        zorder=1,
    )

    axes[2].plot(
        [10, 11],
        [initial_history["val_dices"][-1], resumed_dice_continuation[0]],
        color="red",
        linewidth=2,
        linestyle="--",
        alpha=0.7,
        label="Continuation",
    )

    axes[2].set_title("Validation Dice", fontsize=14)
    axes[2].set_xlabel("Epoch")
    axes[2].set_ylabel("Dice")
    axes[2].legend()
    axes[2].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        f"{out_folder}/clear_training_comparison.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight"
    )
    plt.show()

    # Print explanation
    print("\n" + "=" * 60)
    print("CHECKPOINT RESUME VISUALIZATION EXPLANATION")
    print("=" * 60)
    print("• Blue line: Initial training (epochs 1-10)")
    print("• Orange line: Resumed training continuation (epochs 11-20)")
    print("• Green line: Weights-only training (fresh epochs 1-15)")
    print("• Red dashed line: Shows seamless continuation")
    print()
    print("NOTE: In the original overlapping plot, the blue line was")
    print("completely covered by the orange line because resumed training")
    print("includes the exact same first 10 epochs plus 10 additional epochs.")
    print("=" * 60)

    # Print summary statistics
    print("\nTraining Summary:")
    for name, history in histories.items():
        final_iou = history["val_ious"][-1]
        final_dice = history["val_dices"][-1]
        final_loss = history["train_losses"][-1]
        epochs_trained = len(history["train_losses"])
        print(f"{name}: {epochs_trained} epochs")
        print(f"  Final IoU: {final_iou:.4f}")
        print(f"  Final Dice: {final_dice:.4f}")
        print(f"  Final Loss: {final_loss:.4f}")
        print()

    print("Training comparison plot saved to clear_training_comparison.png")
else:
    print("No training histories found for comparison")

docs.title: Load Checkpoint and Resume Training for Semantic Segmentation Model¶
docs.text:
This notebook tests the new checkpoint loading and resume training functionality for the train_segmentation_model function. It demonstrates how to:

  1. Train a model for a few epochs
  2. Stop training and save a checkpoint
  3. Resume training from the checkpoint
  4. Load only model weights without resuming training state

docs.title: Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: We'll use the same dataset as the main segmentation example.

docs.title: Initial Training (First 10 epochs)¶
docs.text: First, we'll train a model for just 10 epochs and save checkpoints.

docs.title: Resume Training from Checkpoint¶
docs.text: Now we'll resume training from the checkpoint, continuing for another 10 epochs (total 20 epochs).

docs.title: Load Model Weights Only (No Training State Resume)¶
docs.text: Finally, we'll test loading only the model weights without resuming the training state.

docs.title: Load Best Model for Inference¶
docs.text: Test loading the best model for inference.

docs.title: Compare Training Histories¶
docs.text: Let's compare the training histories to verify that resuming worked correctly.

docs.title: Summary¶
docs.text:
This notebook demonstrates the new checkpoint functionality:

  1. Initial Training: Trained for 10 epochs and saved checkpoints
  2. Resume Training: Successfully resumed from epoch 10 and continued to epoch 20
  3. Weights Only: Loaded model weights but started training from epoch 0
  4. Inference: Used the resumed model for inference

The key features tested:

  • checkpoint_path: Path to the checkpoint file
  • resume_training=True: Resume training state (epoch, optimizer, scheduler, metrics)
  • resume_training=False: Load only model weights

This functionality is useful for:

  • Long training jobs that might be interrupted
  • Experimenting with different training parameters after initial training
  • Transfer learning from partially trained models

docs.title: Regularization¶
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to regularize building footprints using the regularize function from the geoai package. The function is a wrapper around the regularize_geodataframe function from the buildingregulariser package. Credits to the original author, Nick Wright.

It can be used to regularize features such as building footprints, solar panels, cars, and other objects that have a regular shape.

docs.title: Train a Land Cover Classification Model¶
Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Create training data¶
docs.text:
We will use the NAIP dataset for land cover classification. The classification scheme is adopted from the Chesapeake Land Cover project.

Important Note for Land Cover Classification:

  • Your label images should contain integer class values (0, 1, 2, ..., 13 for 13 classes)
  • Do NOT use binary masks - the training code now properly handles multi-class labels
  • Class 0 is typically background, classes 1-12 are your land cover types

docs.title: Train semantic segmentation model¶
docs.text:
Now we'll train a semantic segmentation model using the new train_object_detection function. This function supports various architectures from segmentation-models-pytorch:

  • Architectures: unet, deeplabv3, deeplabv3plus, fpn, pspnet, linknet, manet
  • Encoders: resnet34, resnet50, efficientnet-b0, mobilenet_v2, etc.

docs.title: Run inference¶
docs.text: Now we'll use the trained model to make predictions on the test image.

docs.title: Train a Semantic Segmentation Model using Segmentation-Models-PyTorch¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to train semantic segmentation models for object detection (e.g., building detection) using the segmentation-models-pytorch library. Unlike instance segmentation with Mask R-CNN, this approach treats the task as pixel-level binary classification.

docs.title: Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: We'll use the same dataset as the Mask R-CNN example for consistency.

docs.title: Create training data¶
docs.text: We'll create the same training tiles as before.

docs.title: Train semantic segmentation model¶
docs.text:
Now we'll train a semantic segmentation model using the new train_segmentation_model function. This function supports various architectures from segmentation-models-pytorch:

  • Architectures: unet, unetplusplus deeplabv3, deeplabv3plus, fpn, pspnet, linknet, manet
  • Encoders: resnet34, resnet50, efficientnet-b0, mobilenet_v2, etc.

For more details, please refer to the segmentation-models-pytorch documentation.

docs.title: Run inference¶
docs.text: Now we'll use the trained model to make predictions on the test image.

docs.title: Vectorize masks¶
docs.text: Convert the predicted mask to vector format for better visualization and analysis.

docs.title: Model Performance Analysis¶
docs.text: Let's examine the training curves and model performance:

docs.title: Performance Metrics¶
docs.text: IoU (Intersection over Union) and Dice score are both popular metrics used to evaluate the similarity between two binary masks—often in image segmentation tasks. While they are related, they are not the same.

docs.title: IoU (Jaccard Index)¶
docs.text:
$$ \text{IoU} = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} $$

  • Measures the overlap between predicted region $A$ and ground truth region $B$ relative to their union.
  • Ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

docs.title: Dice Score (F1 Score for Sets)¶
docs.text:
$$ \text{Dice} = \frac{2|A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|} $$

  • Measures the overlap between $A$ and $B$, but gives more weight to the intersection.
  • Also ranges from 0 to 1.

docs.title: 🔸 Key Differences¶
docs.text:
Metric Formula Penalizes Sensitivity IoU $\frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ FP and FN equally Less sensitive to small objects Dice $\frac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ Less harsh on small mismatches More sensitive to small overlaps

TP: True Positive, FP: False Positive, FN: False Negative

docs.title: 🔸 Relationship¶
docs.text:
Dice and IoU are mathematically related:

$$ \text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Dice}}{2 - \text{Dice}} $$

docs.title: 🔸 When to Use What¶
docs.text:
• IoU: Common in object detection and semantic segmentation benchmarks (e.g., COCO, Pascal VOC).
  • Dice: Preferred in medical imaging and when class imbalance is an issue, due to its sensitivity to small regions.

docs.title: Train a Semantic Segmentation Model for Water Detection¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to train semantic segmentation models for water detection.

docs.title: Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: We'll use the waterbody dataset from Kaggle. You will need to create an account and download the dataset. I have already downloaded the dataset and saved a copy on Hugging Face. Let's download the dataset:

docs.title: Train semantic segmentation model¶
docs.text:
Now we'll train a semantic segmentation model using the new train_segmentation_model function. This function supports various architectures from segmentation-models-pytorch:

  • Architectures: unet, unetplusplus deeplabv3, deeplabv3plus, fpn, pspnet, linknet, manet
  • Encoders: resnet34, resnet50, efficientnet-b0, mobilenet_v2, etc.

For more details, please refer to the segmentation-models-pytorch documentation.

Let's train the module using U-Net with ResNet34 encoder:

docs.title: Evaluate the model¶
docs.text: Let's examine the training curves and model performance:

docs.title: Run inference¶
docs.text: You can run inference on a new image using the semantic_segmentation function. I don't have a new image to test on, so I'll use one of the training images. In reality, you would use your own images not used in training.

docs.title: Water Detection with Sentinel-2 Imagery¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to train semantic segmentation models for water detection using Sentinel-2 imagery.

docs.title: Install packages¶
docs.text: To use the new functionality, ensure the required packages are installed.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: We'll use the Earth Surface Water Dataset from Zenodo. Credits to the author (Xin Luo) of the dataset

docs.title: Create training data¶
docs.text: We'll create the same training tiles as before.

docs.title: Train semantic segmentation model¶
docs.text:
Now we'll train a semantic segmentation model using the new train_segmentation_model function. This function supports various architectures from segmentation-models-pytorch:

  • Architectures: unet, unetplusplus deeplabv3, deeplabv3plus, fpn, pspnet, linknet, manet
  • Encoders: resnet34, resnet50, efficientnet-b0, mobilenet_v2, etc.

For more details, please refer to the segmentation-models-pytorch documentation.

Let's train the module using U-Net with ResNet34 encoder:

docs.title: Evaluate the model¶
docs.text: Let's examine the training curves and model performance:

docs.title: Visualizing Raster Data with Leafmap¶
docs.text: This notebook demonstrates how to visualize raster data using leafmap. Leafmap can visualize raster data (e.g., Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF) stored in a local file or on the cloud (e.g., AWS S3). It can also visualize raster data stored in a STAC catalog.

docs.title: Installation¶
docs.text: Uncomment the following line to install the required packages if needed.

docs.title: COG¶
docs.text:
A Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF (COG) is a regular GeoTIFF file, aimed at being hosted on a HTTP file server, with an internal organization that enables more efficient workflows on the cloud. It does this by leveraging the ability of clients issuing HTTP GET range requests to ask for just the parts of a file they need. More information about COG can be found at https://www.cogeo.org/in-depth.html

For this demo, we will use data from https://www.maxar.com/open-data/california-colorado-fires for mapping California and Colorado fires. Let's create an interactive map and add the COG to the map.

docs.title: Local Raster¶
docs.text: Leafmap can also visualize local GeoTIFF files. Let's download some sample data

docs.title: STAC¶
docs.text: The SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog (STAC) specification provides a common language to describe a range of geospatial information so that it can more easily be indexed and discovered. A SpatioTemporal Asset is any file that represents information about the earth captured in a certain space and time. STAC aims to enable that next generation of geospatial search engines, while also supporting web best practices so geospatial information is more easily surfaced in traditional search engines. More information about STAC can be found at the STAC website. In this example, we will use a STAC item from the SPOT Orthoimages of Canada available through the link below:

docs.title: Vector viz
docs.text:
In [ ]: Copied! 
# %pip install "leafmap[vector]"
# %pip install "leafmap[vector]" In [ ]: Copied! 
import leafmap
import leafmap In [ ]: Copied! 
m = leafmap.Map(center=[0, 0], zoom=2)
data = "https://opengeos.org/data/vector/cables.geojson"
m.add_vector(data, layer_name="Cable lines", info_mode="on_hover")
m
m = leafmap.Map(center=[0, 0], zoom=2) data = "https://opengeos.org/data/vector/cables.geojson" m.add_vector(data, layer_name="Cable lines", info_mode="on_hover") m

You can style the vector with custom style callback functions.

In [ ]: Copied! 
m = leafmap.Map(center=[20, 0], zoom=2)
m.add_basemap("CartoDB.DarkMatter")
data = "https://opengeos.org/data/vector/cables.geojson"
callback = lambda feat: {"color": feat["properties"]["color"], "weight": 1}
m.add_vector(data, layer_name="Cable lines", style_callback=callback)
m

docs.title: Installation¶
docs.text: Uncomment the following line to install leafmap if needed.

docs.title: Visualize vector data¶
docs.text: You can visualize vector data using the add_vector function. It supports common vector data formats, including GeoJSON, Shapefile, GeoPackage, and any other formats supported by geopandas.

docs.title: Choropleth map¶
docs.text: You can create a choropleth map using the add_data function. It supports GeoJSON, Shapefile, GeoPackage, and any other formats supported by geopandas.

docs.title: GeoParquet¶
docs.text: Visualize GeoParquet data with leafmap and lonboard.

docs.title: PMTiles¶
docs.text: PMTiles is a single-file archive format for tiled data. A PMTiles archive can be hosted on a commodity storage platform such as S3, and enables low-cost, zero-maintenance map applications that are "serverless" - free of a custom tile backend or third party provider.

docs.title: Remote PMTiles¶
docs.text: Leafmap can visualize PMTiles hosted locally or remotely.

docs.title: Source Cooperative¶
docs.text:
Visualize the Google-Microsoft Open Buildings data hosted on Source Cooperative.

Check the metadata of the PMTiles.

docs.title: Local PMTiles¶
docs.text:
tippecanoe is required to convert vector data to pmtiles. Install it with conda install -c conda-forge tippecanoe.

Download building footprints of Derna, Libya.

docs.title: Semantic segmentation
docs.text:

import os

from rastervision.core.data import ClassConfig, SemanticSegmentationLabels

import albumentations as A

    SemanticSegmentationRandomWindowGeoDataset,
    SemanticSegmentationSlidingWindowGeoDataset,
    SemanticSegmentationVisualizer,
    SemanticSegmentationGeoDataConfig,
    SemanticSegmentationLearnerConfig,
    SolverConfig,
    SemanticSegmentationLearner,
)

    class_names=class_config.names, class_colors=class_config.colors
)

data_augmentation_transform = A.Compose(
    [
        A.Flip(),
        A.ShiftScaleRotate(),
        A.OneOf(
            [
                A.HueSaturationValue(hue_shift_limit=10),
                A.RGBShift(),
                A.ToGray(),
                A.ToSepia(),
                A.RandomBrightness(),
                A.RandomGamma(),
            ]
        ),
        A.CoarseDropout(max_height=32, max_width=32, max_holes=5),
    ]
)
data_augmentation_transform = A.Compose( [ A.Flip(), A.ShiftScaleRotate(), A.OneOf( [ A.HueSaturationValue(hue_shift_limit=10), A.RGBShift(), A.ToGray(), A.ToSepia(), A.RandomBrightness(), A.RandomGamma(), ] ), A.CoarseDropout(max_height=32, max_width=32, max_holes=5), ] ) In [ ]: Copied!

    size_lims=(150, 200),

    max_windows=400,
    transform=data_augmentation_transform,
)

len(train_ds)

docs.title: Using the Segment-Geospatial Python Package with ArcGIS Pro¶
docs.text: The notebook shows step-by-step instructions for using the Segment Anything Model (SAM) with ArcGIS Pro. Check out the YouTube tutorial here and the Resources for Unlocking the Power of Deep Learning Applications Using ArcGIS. Credit goes to Esri.

docs.title: Installation¶
docs.text:
1. Open Windows Registry Editor (regedit.exe) and navigate to Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem. Change the value of LongPathsEnabled to 1. See this screenshot. This is a known issue with the deep learning libraries for ArcGIS Pro 3.1. A future release might fix this issue.

  2. Navigate to the Start Menu -> All apps -> ArcGIS folder, then open the Python Command Prompt.

  3. Create a new conda environment and install mamba and Python 3.9.x from the Esri Anaconda channel. Mamba is a drop-in replacement for conda that is mach faster for installing Python packages and their dependencies.

    conda create conda-forge::mamba esri::python --name samgeo

  4. Activate the new conda environment.

    conda activate samgeo

  5. Install arcpy, deep-learning-essentials, segment-geospatial, and other dependencies (~4GB download).

    mamba install arcpy deep-learning-essentials leafmap localtileserver segment-geospatial -c esri -c conda-forge

  6. Activate the new environment in ArcGIS Pro.

    proswap samgeo

  7. Close the Python Command Prompt and open ArcGIS Pro.

  8. Download this notebook and run it in ArcGIS Pro.

docs.title: Download sample data¶
docs.text: In this example, we will use the high-resolution aerial imagery from the USDA National Agricultural Imagery Program (NAIP). You can download NAIP imagery using the USDA Data Gateway or the USDA NCRS Box Drive. I have downloaded some NAIP imagery and clipped them to a smaller area, which are available here.

docs.title: Initialize SAM class¶
docs.text: Specify the file path to the model checkpoint. If it is not specified, the model will to downloaded to the working directory.

docs.title: Automatic mask generation¶
docs.text: Specify the file path to the image we downloaded earlier.

docs.title: Automatic mask generation options¶
docs.text: There are several tunable parameters in automatic mask generation that control how densely points are sampled and what the thresholds are for removing low quality or duplicate masks. Additionally, generation can be automatically run on crops of the image to get improved performance on smaller objects, and post-processing can remove stray pixels and holes. Here is an example configuration that samples more masks:

docs.title: Automatically generating object masks with SAM¶
docs.text:
This notebook shows how to segment objects from an image using the Segment Anything Model (SAM) with a few lines of code.

Make sure you use GPU runtime for this notebook. For Google Colab, go to Runtime -> Change runtime type and select GPU as the hardware accelerator.

The notebook is adapted from segment-anything/notebooks/automatic_mask_generator_example.ipynb, but I have made it much easier to save the segmentation results and visualize them.

docs.title: Segmenting remote sensing imagery with box prompts¶
docs.text:
This notebook shows how to generate object masks from text prompts with the Segment Anything Model (SAM).

Make sure you use GPU runtime for this notebook. For Google Colab, go to Runtime -> Change runtime type and select GPU as the hardware accelerator.

docs.title: Install dependencies¶
docs.text: Uncomment and run the following cell to install the required dependencies.

docs.title: Download a sample image¶
docs.text: Pan and zoom the map to select the area of interest. Use the draw tools to draw a polygon or rectangle on the map

docs.title: Initialize SAM class¶
docs.text:
The initialization of the LangSAM class might take a few minutes. The initialization downloads the model weights and sets up the model for inference.

Specify the file path to the model checkpoint. If it is not specified, the model will to downloaded to the working directory.

Set automatic=False to disable the SamAutomaticMaskGenerator and enable the SamPredictor.

docs.title: Create bounding boxes¶
docs.text: If no rectangles are drawn, the default bounding boxes will be used as follows:

docs.title: Segment the image¶
docs.text: Use the predict() method to segment the image with specified bounding boxes. The boxes parameter accepts a list of bounding box coordinates in the format of [[left, bottom, right, top], [left, bottom, right, top], ...], a GeoJSON dictionary, or a file path to a GeoJSON file.

docs.title: Display the result¶
docs.text: Add the segmented image to the map.

docs.title: Use an existing vector file as box prompts¶
docs.text: Alternatively, you can specify a file path to a vector file. Let's download a sample vector file from GitHub.

docs.title: Segment image with box prompts¶
docs.text: Segment the image using the specified file path to the vector mask.

docs.title: Generating object masks from input prompts with SAM¶
docs.text:
This notebook shows how to generate object masks from input prompts with the Segment Anything Model (SAM).

Make sure you use GPU runtime for this notebook. For Google Colab, go to Runtime -> Change runtime type and select GPU as the hardware accelerator.

The notebook is adapted from segment-anything/notebooks/predictor_example.ipynb, but I have made it much easier to save the segmentation results and visualize them.

docs.title: Install dependencies¶
docs.text: Uncomment and run the following cell to install the required dependencies.

docs.title: Download a sample image¶
docs.text: Pan and zoom the map to select the area of interest. Use the draw tools to draw a polygon or rectangle on the map

docs.title: Initialize SAM class¶
docs.text: Specify the file path to the model checkpoint. If it is not specified, the model will to downloaded to the working directory.

docs.title: Image segmentation with input points¶
docs.text:
A single point can be used to segment an object. The point can be specified as a tuple of (x, y), such as (col, row) or (lon, lat). The points can also be specified as a file path to a vector dataset. For non (col, row) input points, specify the point_crs parameter, which will automatically transform the points to the image column and row coordinates.

Try a single point input:

docs.title: Interactive segmentation¶
docs.text: Display the interactive map and use the marker tool to draw points on the map. Then click on the Segment button to segment the objects. The results will be added to the map automatically. Click on the Reset button to clear the points and the results.

m
m = leafmap.Map(height="600px") m.add_basemap("SATELLITE") m.add_raster("image.tif", layer_name="Image") m.add_layer_manager() m

There are several tunable parameters in automatic mask generation that control how densely points are sampled and what the thresholds are for removing low quality or duplicate masks. Additionally, generation can be automatically run on crops of the image to get improved performance on smaller objects, and post-processing can remove stray pixels and holes. Here is an example configuration that samples more masks:

In [ ]: Copied! 
sam_kwargs = {
    "points_per_side": 32,
    "pred_iou_thresh": 0.86,
    "stability_score_thresh": 0.92,
    "crop_n_layers": 1,
    "crop_n_points_downscale_factor": 2,
    "min_mask_region_area": 80,
}
sam_kwargs = { "points_per_side": 32, "pred_iou_thresh": 0.86, "stability_score_thresh": 0.92, "crop_n_layers": 1, "crop_n_points_downscale_factor": 2, "min_mask_region_area": 80, } In [ ]: Copied! 
sam = SamGeo(
    model_type="vit_h",
    sam_kwargs=sam_kwargs,
)
sam = SamGeo( model_type="vit_h", sam_kwargs=sam_kwargs, ) In [ ]: Copied! 
sam.generate("image.tif", output="mask.tif", foreground=True)
sam.generate("image.tif", output="mask.tif", foreground=True) In [ ]: Copied! 
raster_to_vector("mask.tif", output="mask.shp")
raster_to_vector("mask.tif", output="mask.shp") In [ ]: Copied!

sam.show_masks(cmap="binary_r")

docs.title: Segmenting satellite imagery from the Maxar Open Data Program¶
docs.text:
This notebook shows how to segment satellite imagery from the Maxar Open Data program for Libya floods.

Make sure you use GPU runtime for this notebook. For Google Colab, go to Runtime -> Change runtime type and select GPU as the hardware accelerator.

docs.title: Mapping swimming pools with text prompts¶
docs.text:
This notebook shows how to map swimming pools with text prompts and the Segment Anything Model (SAM).

Make sure you use GPU runtime for this notebook. For Google Colab, go to Runtime -> Change runtime type and select GPU as the hardware accelerator.

docs.title: Install dependencies¶
docs.text: Uncomment and run the following cell to install the required dependencies.

docs.title: Download a sample image¶
docs.text: Pan and zoom the map to select the area of interest. Use the draw tools to draw a polygon or rectangle on the map

docs.title: Initialize LangSAM class¶
docs.text: The initialization of the LangSAM class might take a few minutes. The initialization downloads the model weights and sets up the model for inference.

docs.title: Segment the image¶
docs.text:
Part of the model prediction includes setting appropriate thresholds for object detection and text association with the detected objects. These threshold values range from 0 to 1 and are set while calling the predict method of the LangSAM class.

box_threshold: This value is used for object detection in the image. A higher value makes the model more selective, identifying only the most confident object instances, leading to fewer overall detections. A lower value, conversely, makes the model more tolerant, leading to increased detections, including potentially less confident ones.

text_threshold: This value is used to associate the detected objects with the provided text prompt. A higher value requires a stronger association between the object and the text prompt, leading to more precise but potentially fewer associations. A lower value allows for looser associations, which could increase the number of associations but also introduce less precise matches.

Remember to test different threshold values on your specific data. The optimal threshold can vary depending on the quality and nature of your images, as well as the specificity of your text prompts. Make sure to choose a balance that suits your requirements, whether that's precision or recall.

docs.title: Visualize the results¶
docs.text: Show the result with bounding boxes on the map.

docs.title: Object Detection from Remote Sensing Imagery with GeoAI¶
docs.text:
• 📓 Notebook: https://opengeoai.org/workshops/GeoAI_Workshop_2025
  • 💻 GitHub: https://github.com/opengeos/geoai

docs.title: 🧭 Introduction¶
docs.text: This notebook provides hands-on materials for using the GeoAI package for object detection in remote sensing imagery.

docs.title: 🗂️ Agenda¶
docs.text:
The workshop will guide you through the full pipeline of GeoAI for object detection in remote sensing imagery, including:

  • 📦 Package installation
  • ⬇️ Data download
  • 🖼️ Data visualization
  • 🧠 Model training
  • 🔍 Model inference
  • 🛠️ Post-processing
  • 🌐 Real-world applications

docs.title: ⚙️ Prerequisites¶
docs.text:
• A Google Colab account (recommended for ease of setup)
  • Basic familiarity with Python and geospatial data

docs.title: 📦 Package installation¶
docs.text: You can install the required packages using either conda or pip:

docs.title: Option 1: Using Conda (recommended for local environments)¶
docs.text:
conda create -n geoai python=3.12
conda activate geoai
conda install -c conda-forge mamba
mamba install -c conda-forge geoai

docs.title: Option 2: Using pip (for Colab or quick installation)¶
Retrieve collections¶
docs.text: Get all STAC collections from Microsoft Planetary Computer.

docs.title: Download sample datasets from Hugging Face¶
🌐 Real-world applications¶
docs.text: The section demonstrates how to apply pre-trained models to real-world scenarios.
